project_title: Historical Studies - eleven historical and operational case studies, including the manuscript Trilogy
product_name: Historical Studies - eleven historical and operational case studies, including the manuscript Trilogy
runtime_target: local-first web application or desktop module with persistent project state
operational_end_state: A researcher can enter a project, use named instruments, observe state changes, and export a governed work product.
core_modules: state model | instrument action | before/after trace | export gate
source_strategy: Use sources to strengthen cross-field mechanism transfer and academic-stage dependency. Sources should not merely answer a query; they should help move a mechanism from one field into another while marking downstream stages stale when upstream assumptions change.
source_requirement: Title-level routing can strengthen project planning and proof design. Public claims require project evidence or passage-level source confirmation.
source_gap: passage-level close reading still required before quotation, page-specific citation, or strong literature-positioning claim
test_suite: claim ceiling test: public claims cannot exceed built evidence | source test: metadata-routed sources require close reading before citation or quotation | privacy test: no local paths or private directories in public deploy | state-transition test: visible input changes recorded state and output
current_build_status: specified proof plan; needs live or replayable demonstrator
proof_to_full_build_gap: Build the first runnable or inspectable demo rather than leaving the project at protocol level.
first_full_build: Add a replayable demo with before/after state, failure mode, and exported result so the software utility is visible rather than asserted.
next_implementation_steps: 1. create or confirm runtime data schema; 2. implement one end-to-end fixture; 3. capture proof artifact; 4. run source and claim-ceiling gates; 5. publish reviewer-safe demo
claim_boundary: This is a full-build plan, not a claim that the full operational system has already been completed.
